title: Mystery Stain on Polyester
stainChemistry: Mystery stains have unknown composition and origin. On polyester, the stain could be protein, tannin, oil, dye, or any combination. Misidentifying the stain family leads to wrong treatment — and potentially setting the stain permanently (e.g., hot water on hidden protein, alkali on hidden tannin). Systematic testing is required.
whyThisWorks: The systematic approach on polyester: start with the safest, most universal treatment (cold water + NSD) to avoid setting any component. Then test: does cold water dissolve it (water-soluble)? Does it feel greasy (oil)? Is it stiff/crusty (protein)? Is it colored without texture (dye/tannin)? Once identified, apply the correct family protocol. When in doubt, professional assessment prevents damage.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Test with cold water first — if it dissolves, likely water-soluble (tannin or fresh protein)
2. If greasy texture: start dry side — apply POG, then NSD
3. If stiff/crusty: treat as protein — apply Protein Formula (enzyme-safe fibers) or NSD (delicates)
4. If colored/flat: treat as tannin — apply Tannin Formula, then H2O2 6% on colorfast
5. Flush with NSD after any treatment. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Remove loose material — brush, blot, or scrape as appropriate
2. Apply Dawn dish soap mixed with cold water
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat if needed
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If mystery stain stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is mystery stain on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
